skill.name: blocklet-converter
skill.description: Converts static web and Next.js projects into ArcBlock blocklets using a provided DID, generated configuration, assets, and validation steps.
skill.summary: Prepare static web and Next.js projects for ArcBlock blocklet packaging.
skill.author: ArcBlock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical dangerous combination, but review found no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection. Most weak cryptography, environment, and network alerts are contextual false positives; the real concern is documented command execution for dependency installation, builds, and blocklet bundling on user projects.

medium_findings.title: Project-Controlled Command Execution During Conversion
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The workflow instructs the assistant to install dependencies, run builds, inspect blocklet metadata, and create bundles. This is legitimate for conversion, but dependency lifecycle scripts and build scripts can execute arbitrary project code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and central to the workflow. The risk depends on the target project contents, so this is a legitimate but elevated operational risk.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is Limited to Blocklet Runtime Configuration
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The entry script reads BLOCKLET_MODE, BLOCKLET_APP_DIR, and BLOCKLET_PORT to configure a Next.js server. No evidence found that secrets are collected, logged, or transmitted.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced variables are normal runtime configuration values. The reviewed file does not contain exfiltration logic or broad environment dumping.

low_findings.title: Network Indicators Are Deployment Configuration
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded bind address starts the bundled Next.js server, and the URL points to the ArcBlock store in a template. No evidence found of outbound data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network values appear in server and package metadata contexts. They are not paired with credential reads or requests that send user data.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Metadata False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited lines contain descriptions, DID placeholders, or template metadata. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found text and YAML fields, not cryptographic API use. The scanner likely matched short tokens in template content.

low_findings.title: Template Literal and Parser Alerts Are Not Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The parser uses a regular expression and JSON parsing on marked output, and the examples are documentation. No evidence found of Ruby backticks or shell execution in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited JavaScript backtick is a sample string, not a shell primitive. The parser only extracts a marked JSON payload from text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project-Controlled Command Execution During Conversion
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The workflow instructs the assistant to install dependencies, run builds, inspect blocklet metadata, and create bundles. This is legitimate for conversion, but dependency lifecycle scripts and build scripts can execute arbitrary project code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and central to the workflow. The risk depends on the target project contents, so this is a legitimate but elevated operational risk.

content.user_title: Convert Web Projects to ArcBlock Blocklets
content.value_statement: Packaging a web project as a blocklet requires several ArcBlock files and validation steps. This skill guides conversion for static sites and Next.js apps using a provided DID.
actual_capabilities: Checks whether a project looks like a static web app or Next.js app.
Requires a provided blocklet DID before conversion can proceed.
Extracts project title and description from package metadata.
Creates or updates README, logo, entry point, and blocklet.yml assets.
Runs build and blocklet validation commands when the project supports them.
Stops with specific error messages for unsupported or invalid projects.
limitations: It does not support backend applications or server framework deployments.
It requires the ArcBlock blocklet CLI to be installed separately.
It can run project install and build scripts, so source projects must be trusted.
It needs a valid DID from the user and does not generate one itself.

use_cases.title: Package a Vite or React Site
use_cases.description: Convert a built static web app into a blocklet with the expected manifest, logo, and README files.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Next.js App
use_cases.description: Add the required Next.js entry point and blocklet configuration for ArcBlock packaging.

use_cases.title: Validate Release Readiness
use_cases.description: Run blocklet metadata and bundle checks before handing a web app to an ArcBlock deployment flow.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Current Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this web project to an ArcBlock blocklet using DID z8iaExampleDidValue. Stop if the project is unsupported.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Before Converting
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether this project can become a blocklet. Tell me the detected project type and any blocker before changing files.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a Next.js App
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare this Next.js project as a blocklet using DID z8iaExampleDidValue. Add the required entry point and validate the bundle.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Conversion Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the generated blocklet files and build output. Identify missing metadata, invalid paths, and bundle errors before release.

output_examples.input: A Vite React project with a valid DID and a working build script.
output_examples.output: The skill reports a static web app, emits project title and description, creates blocklet files, validates metadata, and bundles the release.

output_examples.input: A plain HTML project with no README or logo file.
output_examples.output: The skill creates default supporting assets, points the blocklet manifest at the static entry point, and verifies the package.

output_examples.input: A project with backend framework dependencies.
output_examples.output: The skill stops before conversion and explains that backend projects are outside its supported scope.

best_practices: Run it only on trusted project repositories because install and build scripts may execute code.
Provide a valid DID before starting so generated blocklet metadata stays consistent.
Review generated blocklet.yml, README, and logo files before publishing the bundle.
anti_patterns: Do not run it on untrusted projects without sandboxing the install and build steps.
Do not use it for backend services or projects that require custom server deployment.
Do not publish the generated bundle without checking metadata, paths, and branding.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a DID?
faq.answer: No. It requires you to provide a pre-generated blocklet DID before conversion starts.

faq.question: Which project types are supported?
faq.answer: It supports static web apps and Next.js projects. It stops when backend frameworks are detected.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow can run dependency installation, build, metadata, and bundle commands.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The commands are legitimate for packaging, but project install and build scripts can execute arbitrary code.

faq.question: What files can it generate?
faq.answer: It can prepare README, logo, Next.js entry point, and blocklet.yml files based on templates.

faq.question: Can it fix build failures automatically?
faq.answer: It reports build errors and stops. The project owner must fix source or configuration problems.
